content_en:
Taqwa — rendered variously as piety, God-consciousness, fear of Allah, or mindfulness of Allah — is the concept that the Quran and Sunnah identify most frequently as the criterion of true standing before Allah. 'The most honored of you before Allah is the most pious of you' (Quran 49:13) — this verse establishes that in Islam, the hierarchy that matters is not of wealth, lineage, beauty, or intelligence, but of taqwa. Al-Nawawi's chapter on this subject is one of the richest in Riyadh al-Salihin, drawing together hadiths that illuminate the nature, the manifestations, and the fruits of genuine God-consciousness.

The Prophet ﷺ, when asked about taqwa by a Companion, pointed to his chest three times and said: 'Taqwa is here.' (Muslim.) This response locates taqwa as an interior reality — not merely the performance of external acts of worship but the quality of a heart that is genuinely oriented toward Allah. A person may perform all the outward acts of Islamic practice while their heart is occupied with the love of this world and the desire for social recognition; they have the form of taqwa without the substance. And a person may perform acts of piety that are small in number but emerge from a heart genuinely alive to the presence of Allah; their taqwa is genuine.

The chapter includes the Prophet's ﷺ definition of righteousness and sin: 'Al-birr (righteousness) is good character (husn al-khuluq). And ithm (sin) is what itches in your chest and you dislike that people should know about it.' (Muslim.) This definition of sin as what the God-conscious heart recognizes as wrong — even when there is no social prohibition — is a remarkable testament to the role of taqwa as an internal moral compass. The person of taqwa does not need an external list of prohibitions for every situation; their developed God-consciousness generates a sensitivity to right and wrong that guides them even in novel situations.

Al-Nawawi also includes the hadith: 'Leave what makes you doubt for what does not make you doubt.' (Al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i.) This principle of moral cautiousness (al-wara') is closely related to taqwa — the God-conscious person, when in doubt about the permissibility of something, errs on the side of caution rather than seeking every possible justification for what their lower self desires. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'The lawful is clear and the prohibited is clear, and between them are dubious matters that most people do not know about. Whoever guards against dubious matters has protected his religion and his honor.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.)

The chapter's most vivid illustration of taqwa in practice is the famous story of Umar ibn al-Khattab and the shepherd boy. When Umar (then Caliph) encountered a young shepherd tending his master's flock and asked him to sell a sheep, the boy replied: 'These are not mine to sell.' Umar then said: 'Tell your master the wolf ate one.' The boy looked up and said: 'Then where is Allah?' — meaning: Allah sees everything, and I cannot lie even to the Caliph of Islam. Umar wept and purchased the boy's freedom, seeing in that child the taqwa that the most powerful man on earth sought to cultivate in himself.
